question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionSubmit()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the only guard in ConstructionSubmit() (the IsOnline() check at line 336) by targeting an online node, then submit a transaction whose signatures were never verified against the actual account owner's keys, causing hiero.TransactionExecute() to forward the unauthorized transaction to the Hiero network?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionSubmit()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a hex-encoded SignedTransaction via ConstructionSubmit() where the transaction body was constructed to debit a victim account, and the signature was generated by the attacker's own key (accepted by ConstructionCombine()), causing the service to log and execute the transaction without any check that the signing key is authorized for the victim account?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionSubmit()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.TransactionGetTransactionID() at line 351 uses _ to discard errors, meaning a malformed transaction ID does not halt execution, and the transaction is still passed to hiero.TransactionExecute() at line 355, potentially submitting a transaction with a spoofed or missing payer identity?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionHash()] Can an unprivileged external user call ConstructionHash() with a SignedTransaction that contains an attacker-generated signature over a victim's transaction body, causing the service to return a valid transaction hash without any check that the transaction was authorized by the account owner, giving the attacker a hash they can use to track or reference an unauthorized transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionParse()] Can an unprivileged external user call ConstructionParse() with request.Signed = true and a transaction body that lists a victim account as a sender, causing the service to return AccountIdentifierSigners containing the victim's account at line 205, which a downstream client may interpret as proof that the victim has authorized the transaction, even though no actual signature verification occurs in ConstructionParse()?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionParse()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a transaction hex to ConstructionParse() where the transaction body encodes a victim as the payer (TransactionID.AccountID), causing Parse() in the transaction handler to return the victim's AccountId in the accounts slice, and the response to list the victim as an AccountIdentifierSigner without any cryptographic proof of their consent?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionParse()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a signed transaction hex where the embedded signatures are from attacker-controlled keys, submit it to ConstructionParse() with request.Signed = true, and receive a response listing those attacker keys' accounts as AccountIdentifierSigners, creating a false appearance of multi-party authorization that could deceive downstream systems?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionMetadata()] Can an unprivileged external user call ConstructionMetadata() with options[optionKeyAccountAliases] set to a victim's alias, causing resolveAccountAliases() at line 159 to look up the victim's account ID via accountRepo.GetAccountId() and embed it in the returned metadata as account_map, which the attacker then uses in a subsequent ConstructionPayloads() call to set the victim as payer without the victim's authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: resolveAccountAliases()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a comma-separated list of victim account aliases in options[optionKeyAccountAliases], causing resolveAccountAliases() at line 575 to iterate and resolve each alias to its numeric account ID via accountRepo.GetAccountId(), and return a complete alias-to-ID mapping that the attacker uses to forge the account_map metadata and impersonate victims as payers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: resolveAccountAliases()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit resolveAccountAliases() to enumerate valid account IDs for arbitrary aliases by submitting ConstructionMetadata requests with different optionKeyAccountAliases values, using the returned account_map to build a mapping of alias-to-accountId pairs that enables subsequent unauthorized transaction construction targeting those accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getTransactionTimestampProperty()] Can an unprivileged external user set metadataKeyValidUntilNanos to a value just above maxValidDurationNanos (180 * 1e9) in the ConstructionPayloads metadata, causing validStartNanos = validUntil - maxValidDurationNanos at line 407 to compute a near-zero or minimal validStartNanos, creating a transaction with a valid start time in the distant past that may match a previously authorized transaction's time window, enabling replay of prior owner authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getTransactionTimestampProperty()] Can an unprivileged external user supply metadataKeyValidDurationSeconds = 0 in ConstructionPayloads metadata, causing isValidTransactionValidDuration() at line 418 to return true (since 0 >= 0 && 0 <= 180), and then transactionSetValidDuration() at line 745 to default to 180 seconds, creating a maximum-window transaction that extends the time an unauthorized signature remains valid?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getTransactionTimestampProperty()] Can an unprivileged external user set both metadataKeyValidUntilNanos and metadataKeyValidDurationSeconds in the metadata, causing getTransactionTimestampProperty() to ignore validDurationSeconds and validStartNanos (due to the early return at line 414 when validUntilNanos is present), and use only the attacker-controlled validUntilNanos to determine the transaction window, bypassing any intended duration constraints?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: transactionSetTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply validStartNanos = 0 in the metadata, causing transactionSetTransactionId() at line 730 to call hiero.TransactionIDGenerate(payer) which generates a fresh random transaction ID, making it impossible for the account owner to predict or pre-authorize the specific transaction ID, while the attacker controls all other transaction parameters?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: transactionSetTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a specific validStartNanos value via metadata to cause transactionSetTransactionId() at line 733 to call hiero.NewTransactionIDWithValidStart(payer, time.Unix(0, validStartNanos)), setting the payer to a victim account with an attacker-chosen timestamp, creating a deterministic transaction ID that the attacker can predict and use to track unauthorized transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getTransactionNodeAccountId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an arbitrary metadataKeyNodeAccountId string in ConstructionPayloads metadata, causing getTransactionNodeAccountId() at line 383 to parse and use an attacker-chosen node account ID via hiero.AccountIDFromString(), routing the unauthorized transaction to a specific node that may have different validation behavior or be under attacker influence?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionCombine()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ConstructionCombineRequest where the UnsignedTransaction hex encodes a transaction body that was never produced by ConstructionPayloads() (i.e., entirely attacker-crafted), and attach a self-signed signature, causing ConstructionCombine() to accept it since it only checks signature mathematical validity via ed25519.Verify() at line 82 and not the transaction's origin or authorization chain?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionCombine()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getFrozenTransactionBodyBytes() at line 67 extracts BodyBytes via prototext unmarshalling of the transaction string, and that the attacker can craft a transaction proto where BodyBytes encodes a victim's transfer, then sign those bytes with their own key, causing ConstructionCombine() to produce a signed transaction that appears to authorize the victim's funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionCombine()] Can an unprivileged external user provide a Signatures list with a public key that is a valid ed25519 key but corresponds to an account with zero balance or a non-existent account, causing ConstructionCombine() to accept the signature (since ed25519.Verify() at line 82 only checks math) and produce a signed transaction that names a ghost account as the authorizing signer?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionCombine()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ConstructionCombineRequest with a Signatures array containing duplicate entries (same public key, same signature bytes), causing the for loop at line 72 to call hiero.TransactionAddSignature() multiple times with the same key, potentially overwriting or duplicating signature entries in the transaction in a way that confuses downstream signature validation?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionCombine()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a Signatures entry where signature.Bytes is a valid ed25519 signature but was produced over a different transaction's body bytes (not the current frozenBodyBytes), and by also supplying a matching UnsignedTransaction whose frozenBodyBytes happen to match, cause ConstructionCombine() to accept a cross-transaction signature that was never intended to authorize this specific transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionPayloads()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a ConstructionPayloadsRequest with operations that have a zero-value amount for the sender, and since getOperationSlice() at line 432 does not validate amounts, cause transactionHandler.Construct() to build a transaction with unexpected transfer semantics that bypasses the intended authorization model for the account owner?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getOperationSlice()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an operation with operation.Account = nil in a ConstructionPayloadsRequest, causing getOperationSlice() at line 439 to skip account parsing (the nil check at line 439 allows nil accounts), resulting in an operation with a zero-value AccountId that may be interpreted as a system or default account, bypassing owner authorization for that account?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getOperationSlice()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an operation.Amount = nil in a ConstructionPayloadsRequest, causing getOperationSlice() at line 449 to skip amount parsing (the nil check allows nil amounts), resulting in an operation with a nil Amount that may be handled differently by transactionHandler.Construct(), potentially constructing a transaction that omits the sender's debit and bypasses their authorization requirement?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/construction_service.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: ConstructionPayloads()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a ConstructionPayloadsRequest where request.Metadata[types.MetadataKeyMemo] contains a crafted string that, when passed to transactionSetMemo() at line 250, causes the transaction memo to encode attacker-controlled data that misleads the account owner about the transaction's purpose when they review it for signing?
